label: fiction
title: The False Alibi

beats.text: A neighbour saw a man 'about his height' at the side fence around the time — in the dark, for a second or two.
beats.reveal_note: A height and a shadow. Honest, thin, and it never claimed to be more.

beats.text: The prosecution's big moment: under cross-examination, the accused's sister admits the evening she described — the sofa, the film, the takeaway — never happened. The alibi is a lie.
beats.reveal_note: Her lie, invented to spare him — not his. A demolished alibi feels like proof of guilt; it is only proof that someone was frightened enough to lie.

beats.text: Produced late, with an apology to the court: the door log and camera still from a payday-loan office across town — the accused, timestamped, pleading for an extension during the burglary window.
beats.reveal_note: The real alibi, too humiliating to give at first. He preferred 'home with my sister' to 'begging a lender' — and nearly went to prison for his pride.

beats.text: The scene examiner: the shoeprint under the sill is a size 8. The accused takes an 11.
beats.reveal_note: Quietly incompatible. Feet don't lie, and these were three sizes short of his.

beats.text: The judge: a false alibi may be the refuge of the frightened as well as the guilty. It may support a case; it cannot make one.
beats.reveal_note: The direction the instinct needs: the lie collapsed, and with it nothing else — the case still had to stand on its own feet. Size 8 feet.

twist: The alibi was a lie — his sister's, told out of love. The truth was worse to say out loud: he spent the burglary window pleading with a payday lender, on their camera. The instinct that liars are guilty nearly convicted a man whose only crime was shame.
